title: Field configurations for field-free RF trap networks
abstract: We develop a constructive framework for designing radio-frequency (RF) trap networks from planar data and show that non-smooth field-free guide lines are possible in such networks. Given analytic Cauchy data on a symmetry plane, namely the potential and its normal derivative, Laplace's equation determines a local three-dimensional continuation. The odd subclass of this harmonic extension maps an arbitrary analytic generating function $P(x,y)$ to a harmonic potential whose in-plane radio-frequency null set is exactly $P(x,y)=0$. This yields explicit field-free guide networks beyond smooth straight-line intersections, including cusp guides, cotangential contacts, and periodic lattices. We further derive Fourier-space formulas for periodic extensions and present square-lattice network families with tunable local crossing angle and rounded connectivity. These results provide a compact parametrization for the design space for quantum charge-coupled device (QCCD) architectures.
summary: The paper introduces a new method for designing complex radio-frequency trap networks for ion-based quantum computing. By using harmonic extensions, the authors show how to create field-free paths that include non-smooth shapes like cusps and periodic lattices. This provides a new way to parameterize and design scalable quantum charge-coupled device architectures.
main_problem: Designing radio-frequency (RF) trap networks for the Quantum Charge-Coupled Device (QCCD) architecture that allow for field-free guide lines, specifically expanding beyond smooth, straight-line intersections to include non-smooth geometries.
main_result: Developed a constructive framework using harmonic extensions to design RF trap networks with arbitrary field-free guide lines, including cusps, cotangential contacts, and periodic lattices.
method: Uses a harmonic extension of planar Cauchy boundary conditions (potential and normal derivative) and an 'odd subclass' of this extension to map analytic generating functions to 3D potentials.
model_or_system: The system is an ion trap network for Quantum Information Processing (QIP) using a quasi-static RF field where ions are confined by a ponderomotive potential derived from the electric potential.
key_observables: RF null sets (field-free lines) and the ponderomotive potential iso-surfaces.
important_parameters: Typical dimensions in the mm scale, frequencies in the MHz range, and the quasi-static approximation regime.
main_assumptions: Assumes a source-free region and focuses on the 'odd' subclass of the harmonic extension; does not yet address the physical electrode geometry required to produce these fields.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows null lines and ponderomotive potential iso-surfaces for a cusp-line example; Figure 2 illustrates a periodic network of rounded squares with similar potential surfaces.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a framework for designing RF trap networks, presents a mathematical method for harmonic extension of Cauchy data, demonstrates specific non-smooth and periodic geometries, and discusses limitations and future directions.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for AMO physicists working on ion trap QIP, as it provides a new mathematical toolkit for engineering complex, scalable trap architectures (QCCD) with controlled transport properties.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. Null lines and ponderomotive potential iso-surface corresponding to the periodic network introduced in Eq. 13 for c = 1/4.
